title: Coloured clay veneer inlay on air-dry clay
subtitle: Inlaying a thin sheet of tinted clay into a base surface and scraping back to flush
excerpt: A surface decoration where a thin sheet of contrasting-colour clay is pressed into a recess cut into the base clay, then scraped back flush once both have firmed. The result is a two-tone surface with the inlaid pattern in colour, set into a base in another shade. The technique adapts the classical inlaid-clay method to air-dry clay tinted with acrylic.
sourceNotes: Inlay of coloured clay bodies into a contrasting base is a classical decoration technique found in Korean Goryeo celadon and Japanese Mishima ware. The principle of inlay-and-scrape-flush adapts directly to air-dry clay tinted with acrylic. V&A open-access collection for historical inlay reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Decoration where one material is set into a recess in another so the two finish flush at the surface, leaving a colour or texture contrast at the boundary.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape under pressure but is still damp enough to cut and join cleanly. The correct stage for clay veneer inlay.
A thin sheet of one material applied to the surface of another. In clay inlay, the veneer is the tinted sheet pressed into the recess.

content.text: Inlay

of a coloured clay sheet into a base clay surface gives a two-tone pattern that is part of the clay itself rather than painted on. The colour stays in place under any handling because it is physically embedded into the body rather than sitting on top of it. The classical version is Korean Mishima, where white slip lines are set into grey celadon clay before firing. This adaptation uses tinted air-dry clay as the inlay material and scrapes it flush with a metal rib once both layers have firmed.
When to use it

Clay veneer inlay works on flat or gently curved air-dry clay surfaces at the early leather-hard stage. Suits tiles, slab dishes, the flat outer wall of a slab box, the side of a coil-built vase. Sharp inside corners and tight curves are harder to inlay cleanly because the veneer must follow the shape of the recess. The technique is intermediate because it requires reading dryness in two clay pieces at once: the base must be firm enough to hold the cut cleanly, and the veneer must be soft enough to press in without cracking.
What you need
A piece of air-dry clay work at early leather-hard stage, the base for the inlay
A small amount of fresh air-dry clay (about 30 g for a tile-sized inlay), tinted with acrylic paint
A fettling knife for cutting the recess and the veneer
A needle tool for fine adjustments
A metal rib for scraping the inlay flush
Tinting the veneer clay

Take 30 g of fresh air-dry clay and flatten it on the board. Squeeze 5-10 ml of acrylic paint onto the surface and fold the clay in half over the paint. Wedge for two or three minutes, folding and pressing, until the colour is uniform throughout. The clay will be slightly softer than the base because of the added moisture; rest it under plastic for 15 minutes before rolling.
Step-by-step
1. Roll the veneer sheet

Roll the tinted clay between two 2 mm thick slats to an even 2 mm thickness. Keep the sheet large enough that the inlay shape can be cut from it with a clean margin all the way round. Cover the rolled sheet with a damp cloth so it does not dry out before use.
2. Cut the inlay shape from the veneer
Decide on the inlay shape: a small geometric form, a leaf, a circle, a curved band. Cut the shape from the
veneer
sheet with the fettling knife, working slowly and cleanly. Keep the cut piece flat on a board so it does not stretch or deform as you handle it.
3. Trace the shape onto the base

Lay the cut veneer in position on the base clay and lightly outline its edge with the needle tool, scoring a faint guideline. Lift the veneer away and set it aside. The guideline on the base shows where to cut the recess.
4. Cut the recess in the base
With the base clay at
leather-hard

, carve out a recess inside the guideline to a depth of 2 mm. This matches the veneer thickness. Use the fettling knife held at a shallow angle, peeling back small ribbons of clay until the recess floor is flat and the depth is even. Clean up the recess edges with the needle tool so they are vertical and the corners are sharp.
5. Press the veneer into the recess

Lay the cut veneer into the recess and press it down with the flat of a finger, working from the centre outward to expel any air. The veneer should sit slightly proud of the surrounding surface; this excess will be scraped away. If the veneer is too small for the recess, the inlay will have a visible gap at the edge that no amount of scraping will fix.
6. Let both layers firm together
Cover the work loosely with plastic and leave for 30-60 minutes. Both the base and the veneer reach the same stiffness, so they scrape cleanly together rather than one dragging into the other.
7. Scrape the inlay flush

Hold the metal rib flat against the surface and scrape in long even strokes across the whole area, including the boundary between veneer and base. The proud excess of veneer comes away as fine shavings, leaving the inlay flush with the surrounding clay. Continue until the surface reads as one continuous plane with the colour change marking the inlay boundary.
What it looks like when it is right

The finished surface is a single flat plane with the inlay reading as a colour change at the edge, not as a raised pattern. Running a fingertip across the inlay boundary feels no step or seam. The colour of the veneer is crisp at the boundary, not smudged into the base, and the base around the inlay is clean of any drag marks from the scraping.
Common mistakes

Cutting the recess at the wrong dryness. Too soft a base smears under the knife and the recess walls collapse. Too dry a base resists the knife and the edges chip. The right window is firm leather-hard, where the clay cuts cleanly with crisp edges.
Veneer too thin for the recess depth. The scrape pulls the veneer below the base surface, leaving a sunken inlay. Match veneer thickness to recess depth exactly: both at 2 mm.
Scraping while layers are at different dryness. The wetter clay drags into the dryer, smearing the colour boundary. Cover both layers and let them firm together before scraping.

items.symptom: Inlay sits below the base surface after scraping
items.cause: Recess was deeper than the veneer thickness, or the veneer compressed under the scraping pressure
items.fix: Match recess depth to veneer thickness exactly; cut the recess no deeper than the veneer is thick

items.symptom: Colour boundary is blurred or smeared
items.cause: Scraped while the veneer was still wetter than the base, dragging colour into the surrounding clay
items.fix: Cover both layers with plastic for 30-60 minutes before scraping; both should reach the same firm stage together

items.symptom: Gaps visible around the inlay edge
items.cause: Veneer cut slightly smaller than the recess
items.fix: Trace the veneer onto the base before cutting the recess; cut the recess strictly inside the traced line

items.symptom: Veneer cracks as it is pressed in
items.cause: Veneer dried too much before pressing, or rolled too thin
items.fix: Cover the rolled veneer with a damp cloth while preparing the recess; aim for 2 mm thickness, not thinner
